rationales.title: Project as structured data
rationales.narrative:
The master planning doc is an anti-pattern: a 600-line markdown monolith. No part of it can be independently injected into an agent's prompt via template composition. A workflow can't say 'give this agent the architecture and conventions blocks.' It's all or nothing.

The project must be structured the same way everything else in the system is structured — typed JSON blocks that templates compose from. Each block is typed (JSON Schema). Each block is independently injectable into agent prompts via Nunjucks templates. A spec implementation agent gets .workflow/architecture.json + .workflow/conventions.json + .workflow/phases/07-project-structure.json composed into its prompt — exactly the context it needs, not a blob.

Agents don't write to project blocks. An agent outputs results.json. The workflow puts it at outputs/step-name.json. The project incorporates results into its blocks through a separate process. Three layers with clear boundaries:
- Agent: receives composed prompt, does work, outputs JSON. Knows nothing about the project.
- Workflow: orchestrates agents, flows data between steps. Knows nothing about project structure.
- Project: owns the blocks. Produces intent for workflows. Incorporates results. Tracks state.

Intent flows from conversation to project to workflow. Users discuss an idea, refine it, then structure it — that conversation produces intent which gets recorded in project blocks (a phase spec, a decision, a convention). Workflows consume structured intent from project blocks. Results flow back as artifacts. The project advances. New intent emerges. The cycle: conversation → structured intent → workflow → artifacts → project blocks → next intent.

This is sometimes how people work. Discuss. Refine. Then put it into structured form to run workflows upon to produce desired results. The system must support that natural flow — from unstructured conversation to structured project blocks to executable workflows.

rationales.title: Plan decomposition as a templated workflow step
rationales.narrative:
The step between 'phase spec exists' and 'agents are implementing' is plan decomposition — a planner agent that takes composed project blocks and produces informed implementation plans. This step is itself templated and repeatable.

Input (composed from project blocks via templates): Phase spec (intent, success criteria, spec list), Architecture block (current modules, responsibilities, boundaries, line counts), Conventions block (rules, enforcement methods, test/lint commands), Relevant source files (read during investigation by the planner agent).

Investigation (the planner agent does this): Reads source files referenced in architecture block. Identifies existing patterns (how current step types are structured, how tests are written). Maps dependencies between specs (which share files, which are independent). Determines context each implementing agent needs (which source files, which patterns to follow).

Output (typed JSON — plan-breakdown contract): plans[] with name, intent, tasks[], files_to_change[], acceptance_criteria[], context_needed[] (which project blocks and source files to inject), parallel_group (which plans can run concurrently). Parallelism decisions: which specs are independent, which share files, merge order.

What this replaces: the manual process of reading the codebase, understanding patterns, writing implementation specs by hand, and deciding what can parallelize. The planner agent does this investigation, makes these decisions, and produces structured plans that the workflow's forEach step iterates over. Each implementing agent gets exactly the context it needs — composed from project blocks and relevant source files via Nunjucks templates, not a monolithic blob.

The template: the planner agent's task template (templates/plan-decomposer/task.md) renders from typed input — phase spec fields, architecture modules, convention rules. The same template works for any phase. The project blocks change; the decomposition process is the same.

rationales.title: Fresh context, composed detail
rationales.narrative:
Every agent in the pipeline gets a clean context window. No accumulated conversation history, no context bleed between steps. Prior steps produce JSON artifacts to disk. The next agent's task template renders from those artifacts via Nunjucks.

The planner agent's task template renders from project blocks:
{{ architecture.modules }} ← from architecture.json
{{ conventions.rules }} ← from conventions.json
{{ phase.intent }} ← from phases/07.json
{{ phase.success_criteria }} ← from phases/07.json

The Nunjucks template composes these blocks into the prompt. The agent sees structured context about modules, their responsibilities, their line counts, the conventions it must follow, the criteria it must satisfy. It reads the actual source files it needs to understand (via tools). It produces plans.

Then each implementing agent gets another fresh context window. Its task template renders from the planner's output:
{{ plan.intent }} ← from planner's JSON output
{{ plan.tasks }} ← specific to this plan
{{ plan.files_to_change }} ← specific to this plan
{{ architecture.modules }} ← same block, re-rendered
{{ conventions.rules }} ← same block, re-rendered

No context bleed between implementing agents. Each one sees its plan, its files, the architecture, the conventions. Nothing from other plans. Nothing from the conversation that produced the phase spec. The subprocess model makes this possible — each step spawns a separate pi process. Data flows through the file system as typed JSON, not through conversation as accumulated text.

Implementing agent output is a typed JSON artifact. The agent's task template declares the output shape — execution-results contract: tasks completed, decisions made, issues discovered, test counts, commit hashes. No ephemeral prose. No free-flow notes for a human to read and manually route. The agent outputs structured JSON validated against a schema. A downstream workflow step reads that JSON and routes fields to the right project blocks — decisions to .workflow/decisions.json, issues to .workflow/state.json, test count updates, commit hashes. The cycle is automated, typed, and auditable. What was done manually (reading agent notes, copy-pasting into project blocks) is exactly the step the workflow automates.

rationales.title: Audit and conformance infrastructure
rationales.narrative:
Two linked schemas, two instances, connected by principle IDs.

Conformance reference (.workflow/schemas/conformance-reference.schema.json → .workflow/conformance-reference.json): The rules. Project-scoped — a pi extension declares pi principles, a Swift project declares Swift principles. 10 principles, 29 rules, each with check_method (grep/command/ast/inspect), patterns, examples, anti-patterns, severity. For greenfield: author declares directly. For brownfield: analysis agent reads codebase patterns and produces the reference as typed JSON. Either way, same schema, same downstream consumption.

Audit output (.workflow/schemas/audit.schema.json → .workflow/audits/conformance-20260314.json): The findings. Auditor-scoped — different tools produce the same shape. checks[] (deterministic pass/fail), findings[] (with id for dedup, severity, principle linking to reference, category, locations with file + lines, fix with suggestion + verify_method + verify_command/pattern, related IDs clustering findings into single implementation tasks), summary (counts + pass_rate).

The reference is input to the auditor. The audit is the output. Both are typed JSON consumable by templates, transforms, and downstream agents. A workflow composes the reference into the auditor's prompt via Nunjucks, the auditor produces findings against those principles. One schema for all analysis outputs — typed-analysis synthesize output should conform to audit.schema.json too.

This infrastructure enables the programmatic workflow: structured reference → audit → structured findings → merge/prioritize → forEach generate implementation spec → forEach implement → verify. Every step is typed JSON in, typed JSON out, template-composed prompts.

rationales.title: Conversation output → typed project blocks
rationales.narrative:
Design conversations produce ideas that mix concerns: capability gaps, design decisions, implementation plans, architecture changes. The project infrastructure has typed JSON blocks for each concern with schemas. The question is how conversation output flows into these blocks.

Three processes, not one:
1. Gaps, decisions, rationale — append-only array blocks. Main context has the intelligence to extract and classify. /workflow add-work injects schemas as instruction, LLM reads conversation and writes JSON, validation confirms conformance.
2. Phase creation — needs a dedicated agent (phase-author) to decompose intent into specs with acceptance criteria. Already a workflow (create-phase).
3. Architecture refresh — needs codebase analysis, not conversation analysis. Already planned as spec 37.

The key insight: not every project update needs a workflow. Array block appends are LLM intelligence work in main context. Structured decomposition (phases) and analysis (architecture) need fresh-context agents. The mechanism matches the cognitive demand.

rationales.title: The gap between 'work on X' and the system working on X
rationales.narrative:
When the user says 'work on tui-step-visibility', what should happen is: load the gap → read referenced design docs → produce a phase spec → forEach over specs → implement → verify. What actually happens: the human manually reads the gap, reads the doc, formulates a task, delegates to a subagent. The human is the orchestrator.

Three missing pieces:
1. gap-to-phase: a workflow or command that loads a gap by ID, enriches it with referenced details, and feeds it to create-phase.
2. do-phase: a workflow that takes a phase spec, iterates over specs, implements each, verifies against acceptance criteria. Recorded as an open gap, partially blocked by nested-workflow-invocation for the full version but buildable as a flat single-phase flow now.
3. The connection between them: 'work on gap X' → gap-to-phase → do-phase → done.

This is the meta-gap: the system can record what needs doing (gaps, phases, specs) but can't yet execute the full cycle from intent to implementation without human orchestration.

rationales.title: Monitors as peer observers via event bus, not built-in
rationales.narrative:
Two options for workflow observability: build monitoring into the workflow extension, or emit events and let a separate monitor extension observe them.

Building in seems simpler but conflates concerns. Every observation point is workflow-specific (step failures, schema violations, agent output quality), but what to DO with observations is a monitoring concern (write gaps, alert, aggregate). The workflow extension shouldn't decide monitoring policy.

The event bus is the boundary. Workflow emits structured events at key lifecycle points. pi-behavior-monitors (already installed globally) can observe these events and write to .workflow/gaps.json with source:'monitor'. The workflow extension never imports monitor code.

The contract is the event shapes — what a workflow:step-complete carries, what workflow:block-write includes. Defining these shapes is the work. The implementation on both sides is straightforward once the shapes exist.

rationales.title: Deterministic validation at write time vs behavioral observation over time
rationales.narrative:
Two concerns that look similar but have different owners and mechanisms.

Deterministic validation: 'is this JSON valid against its schema?' Runs at write time, in the producing code (workflow extension). AJV, a few lines. If it fails, the write doesn't happen. No LLM, no monitor, no event system. The extension validates its own output.

Behavioral observation: 'this agent keeps producing invalid output' or 'the same finding recurs across runs.' Requires seeing patterns over time, across multiple events. LLM classification is appropriate here — the monitor reads conversation context, matches against a pattern library, steers or records. This is what pi-behavior-monitors is built for.

The mistake would be making validation depend on monitors. That makes it bypassable (don't install monitors → no validation). Validation must be in the write path. Monitors add value on top — detecting trends, learning patterns, steering agents — but the baseline guarantee (valid data in blocks) comes from the producing extension.

The event system (pi.events) is the bridge. Workflow extension emits events at lifecycle points. Monitors can subscribe for richer signal. But the events are additive — monitors work without them (they read conversation context), and validation works without monitors (it's in the write path).

rationales.title: Project and workflow are separate domains, discovered through entanglement
rationales.narrative:
The workflow extension grew to contain two things: an execution engine and a typed knowledge structure. The execution engine (YAML specs, DAG planning, subprocess dispatch, checkpoint/resume) knows nothing about gaps, decisions, or phases. The knowledge structure (.workflow/ blocks, schemas, validation, model-config) knows nothing about step execution or subprocess dispatch.

The entanglement is visible in specific places: /workflow add-work is a project command wearing a workflow hat. route-results is a workflow step that directly manipulates project files. model-config.json is project-level configuration consumed by the workflow engine.

Rather than extract prematurely, we'll build the project layer through use-case modeling — each time we encounter project functionality (add-work, validate, block writes, model resolution), we note what the project extension would own vs what the workflow extension would consume. The boundary emerges from usage, not from architecture diagrams.

The end state: pi-project as a lightweight extension owning .workflow/ (or .project/), registering /project commands, emitting block-write events, providing schema validation. pi-workflows as a peer that consumes project infrastructure for model config and result routing, but runs independently for pure execution.

rationales.title: The standard path from gap to done — fixed workflow, composed intelligence
rationales.narrative:
The question was: given an intent for work to be done, what steps are nearly always necessary? The answer is a fixed workflow with six steps: load the gap, investigate the codebase, research known solutions (in parallel with investigation), decompose findings into specs, implement each spec, verify and route results.

The workflow structure never changes. What changes is the content flowing through it — different gaps produce different investigation findings, different decomposition specs, different implementation tasks. The intelligence lives in the agents and their composed prompts (heuristics, policies, project context via templates), not in the workflow topology.

Key separation: investigation produces understanding (what's the problem), not plans. Decomposition produces plans (what to do about it). These are different cognitive tasks needing different context. An investigator stuffed with planning heuristics wastes context window on the wrong concern. A decomposer that also had to investigate would duplicate work the investigator already did.

Parallelism falls out naturally. Investigation and research have no mutual data dependency — the DAG planner puts them in the same layer. The decompose agent decides which implementation specs are parallel based on file overlap and dependency analysis — known software engineering heuristics composed into its prompt, not hardcoded in the engine.

rationales.title: Prompt Engineering as Infrastructure
rationales.narrative: Agent prompts are infrastructure, not documentation. When the investigator omitted required schema fields, the fix wasn't 'tell the agent to try harder' — it was showing the exact output shape in the prompt. When agents spent 20 minutes on trivial gaps, the fix wasn't 'add a timeout' — it was calibrating the prompt's degree of freedom to task complexity. XML structure, explicit constraints, anti-patterns, and success criteria are not style preferences — they directly determine whether the agent produces valid output on the first attempt. The output schema injection pattern (reading the schema file and rendering it in the template) eliminated the class of errors where prompts and validators disagree. The author-agent-prompts skill codifies these patterns so every new agent starts from proven structure.

rationales.title: Monitor and Workflow Coexistence
rationales.narrative: The fragility monitor and workflow executor share the same filesystem and conversation context but serve different purposes. The monitor observes agent behavior and writes gaps. The executor runs steps and validates block files. When the monitor writes to gaps.json during a workflow step, block validation sees the change and attributes it to the step. Three fixes were needed: (1) fix the monitor's enum values to match the gaps schema, (2) fix block validation to always rollback on failure (not just retries), (3) accept that valid monitor writes during steps are harmless — they pass validation and persist. False positives from the monitor observing subprocess artifacts (truncated output, conversation fragments) remain an open issue but don't break execution.
